judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 better matches the expected early-concurrent/deferred staging (b) by starting all disjoint groups (including F-11's group) in parallel while still serializing registry.py to one owner; Report 2 explicitly delays F-11 to Wave 2 after registry.py's Wave 1, missing that structure. Both reports hit (a) and (c), and both miss (d): Report 2 even surfaces RESULTS_DB but wrongly concludes it poses no collision risk.

report_1.evidence: Group B — pipeline/registry.py: F-02, F-05, F-09**. Three independent one-line constant edits ... give to one engineer as a single small PR rather than splitting three ways — the coordination overhead of three people editing an 11-line file would exceed the work itself.

report_2.evidence: Owner A — pipeline/registry.py: F-02 (add aurora-1 endpoint), F-05 (bump DEFAULT_RUBRIC_VERSION), F-09 (fix DEFAULT_DATASET path). All three are independent constant edits in the same small file — bundling them in one engineer avoids three people touching the same 11-line file.

report_1.evidence: All five groups can start on day one in parallel since none of the fixes textually depend on another group's changes ... The only sequencing constraint is within Group A, and the hold on F-06 pending a decision (Q3).

report_2.evidence: Wave 1 (fully parallel, no shared files, no ambiguity): Owner A (registry.py) and Owner B (prompt files) run first and concurrently ... Wave 2 (area-local application code, still parallel to each other, but each internally sequenced): Owner C (rubric.py) and Owner D (run_batch.py) run concurrently with each other

report_1.evidence: F-06 needs a decision, not just an engineer. ... I would route this to whoever owns the rubric/grading policy (the reviewer, or a product owner) to get explicit new threshold values before assigning it as an implementation task — otherwise an engineer is being asked to invent scoring policy, not fix a bug.

report_2.evidence: this is a product/behavior decision, not a bug fix. The finding names no target values, and PARTIAL_CREDIT_THRESHOLDS = (0.35, 0.65) in pipeline/score.py directly determines every downstream score this pipeline produces. ... I'd surface this to whoever owns the rubric/scoring policy for a concrete target (e.g., "raise low threshold to 0.45") before it becomes a ticket.

tests/conftest.py references RESULTS_DB (defaults to /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db via os.environ.get) but nothing in the current test files actually reads that variable — I could not find any test using it, so I don't believe any engineer needs that path to exist locally for the current suite to run
